answer:
The corpus contains wine and intoxication mentions across 16 works. Here is a full breakdown by work:

**Diwan-E-Ghalib** (mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c): The most extensive coverage — wine, tavern (kharabaat), the Saaqi (wine-bearer), drunkenness, and intoxication appear across dozens of chapters including ch.2, 6, 7, 14, 20, 21, 35, 42, 50, 51, 58, 59, 61, 62, 68, 70, 75, 77, 78, 100, 105, 124, 128, 131, 132, 134, 138, 146, 150, 151, 155, 169, 176, 181, 188, 190, 194, 198, 202, 239. Wine here functions as a Sufi metaphor for divine love alongside literal references.

**Bang-E-Dara Part 1** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c): ch.4 ('wine kept in delicate crystal goblets'), ch.5 ('votary of wine'), ch.9 ('drinking wine is not a sin'), ch.17 ('Passion's wine'; 'drunk on wines of its own mind'), ch.18 ('wine of this brimming chalice'), ch.23 ('wine of knowledge'), ch.25 ('every bud is drunk with life'), ch.28 ('evening's hem is stained with wine'), ch.32 ('drunk on the wine of sleep'), ch.36 ('serve the wine of love'), ch.40 ('wine's effect'), ch.43 ('strange and silent tavern'), ch.44 ('pious wine was filled').

**Bang-E-Dara Part 2** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c): ch.3 ('trade of wine'), ch.4 ('O Wine, how you have loved your hidden state'), ch.5 ('world becomes one tavern, and each soul will be a drunken wight'), ch.8 ('vintage wine be so warm'), ch.11 ('sacred wine's celestial fate'), ch.14 ('drunk on the wine of their Fate'; 'Drunk on the wine of its own creation'), ch.16 ('tavern of morn'), ch.26 ('Love is the purest wine'), ch.28 ('creed of the wine-cup').

**Bang-E-Dara Part 3** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8): ch.4 ('New drinkers, and new wine'; 'drunk on wealth'), ch.7 ('drunk on wine that idle pleasure sings'), ch.11 ('drunk on the wine of devotion'), ch.18 (saaqi and wine references in transliteration), ch.20 ('drunk with their pride').

**Diwan-E-Zauq** (sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798): ch.4 (tavern), ch.15 ('tavern's wine domain'; 'water would turn into wine'), ch.38 ('wine of joy from heaven's tavern'), ch.45 ('give my rival the wine cup'), ch.56 ('tavern has no place'), ch.57 ('do not bolt the tavern door').

**Andreas** (cynewulf-andreas-07b573): ch.1 — 'hallowed wine from water' (miracle), 'wine-halls wasted', 'wine-city' (compound kenning), 'mead-pouring sorrow'.

**Old English Elegies** (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e): ch.1 (mead-hall), ch.2 ('mead-bench...ale-soaked warrior...wine-sated man...mead-mad man' — explicit intoxication causing death), ch.5 ('sipping of mead'), ch.6 ('mead-giving'), ch.7 ('mead-hall'), ch.8 ('mead-halls'; 'mead-joys'), ch.11 ('mead-hall'), ch.14 ('mead-hall'), ch.16 ('mead-hall full of human joys').

**Elene** (cynewulf-elene-d2d132): ch.1 — 'mead-hall he received treasures'.

**Manusmṛti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76): ch.7 — 'intoxication' listed among the tenfold vices of kings ('gambling, sleeping by day, slander, women, intoxication, the triad of song, dance, and music, and aimless wandering').

**Parāśara Smṛti** (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be): ch.10 — a woman 'overcome by intoxication or delusion'.

**Slokantara** (unknown-slokantara-d7a628): ch.1 — explicit discussion of the three causes of intoxication: sura (palm wine), learning/eloquence, and wealth (Lakṣmī).

**Vratisasana** (unknown-vratisasana-d0fe75): ch.1 — multiple passages: intoxication listed as a source of moral failure ('anger comes greed; from greed comes delusion; from delusion comes intoxication; from intoxication comes envy; from envy comes violence'), as a mitigating factor in vows, and as an aggravating factor in violations.

**Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (unknown-yajnavalkya-smtp-cb88d6): ch.2 — 'no punishment for acts done through intoxication, madness, and the like'.

**Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana** (unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a): ch.1 — 'doubt and intoxication' named as causes of a broken understanding.

**Nouveau traité d'économie VOL I** (charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce): ch.12 — workers spending wages at the tavern.

**Traité de Législation VOL I** (charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2): ch.18 — a man spending his income in a tavern, used as a moral/economic example.
